item.title: Multi-Modal Few-Shot Object Detection with Meta-Learning-Based Cross-Modal Prompting
item.dc:creator: Guangxing Han
Long Chen
Jiawei Ma
Shiyuan Huang
Rama Chellappa
Shih-Fu Chang
item.description: We study multi-modal few-shot object detection (FSOD) in this paper, using both few-shot visual examples and class semantic information for detection, which are complementary to each other by definition. Most of the previous works on multi-modal FSOD are fine-tuning-based which are inefficient for online applications. Moreover, these methods usually require expertise like class names to extract class semantic embedding, which are hard to get for rare classes. Our approach is motivated by the high-level conceptual similarity of (metric-based) meta-learning and prompt-based learning to learn generalizable few-shot and zero-shot object detection models respectively without fine-tuning. Specifically, we combine the few-shot visual classifier and text classifier learned via meta-learning and prompt-based learning respectively to build the multi-modal classifier and detection models. In addition, to fully exploit the pre-trained language models, we propose meta-learning-based cross-modal prompting to generate soft prompts for novel classes present in few-shot visual examples, which are then used to learn the text classifier. We introduce a knowledge distillation framework to train the soft prompt generator. This removes the need for human-defined class names during inference, which are often unavailable for rare categories. Our insight is that the few-shot support images naturally include related context information and semantics of the class. We comprehensively evaluate the proposed multi-modal FSOD models on multiple few-shot object detection benchmarks, achieving promising results. Published: 2026-02-22T07:56:13+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.823 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guangxing Han; Long Chen; Jiawei Ma; Shiyuan Huang; Rama Chellappa; Shih-Fu Chang

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-025-02592-3

Relevance: 0.823 (must_read)

We study multi-modal few-shot object detection (FSOD) in this paper, using both few-shot visual examples and class semantic information for detection, which are complementary to each other by definition. Most of the previous works on multi-modal FSOD are fine-tuning-based which are inefficient for online applications. Moreover, these methods usually require expertise like class names to extract class semantic embedding, which are hard to get for rare classes. Our approach is motivated by the high-level conceptual similarity of (metric-based) meta-learning and prompt-based learning to learn generalizable few-shot and zero-shot object detection models respectively without fine-tuning. Specifically, we combine the few-shot visual classifier and text classifier learned via meta-learning and prompt-based learning respectively to build the multi-modal classifier and detection models. In addition, to fully exploit the pre-trained language models, we propose meta-learning-based cross-modal prompting to generate soft prompts for novel classes present in few-shot visual examples, which are then used to learn the text classifier. We introduce a knowledge distillation framework to train the soft prompt generator. This removes the need for human-defined class names during inference, which are often unavailable for rare categories. Our insight is that the few-shot support images naturally include related context information and semantics of the class. We comprehensively evaluate the proposed multi-modal FSOD models on multiple few-shot object detection benchmarks, achieving promising results.

item.title: Cloud removal in remote sensing images using the G2R framework and SSMamba
item.dc:creator: Chengyao Zhang
Fengyan Wang
Xuqing Zhang
Mingchang Wang
Feng Chen
Xiang Wu
item.description: Developing a practical and robust cloud removal method has long been a crucial research objective in the field of remote sensing. Existing multi-temporal approaches typically adopt cloud-free images from other acquisition dates as training targets. However, the inherent temporal heterogeneity of optical observations introduces intrinsic limitations to this paradigm. To overcome these issues, we propose a novel cloud removal strategy based on a Generate-to-Remove (G2R) self-supervised framework. Unlike conventional methods, G2R employs the cloudy image itself as the supervision target, thereby introducing temporal constraints into the reconstruction process and enabling faithful recovery of the original surface features beneath cloud-covered regions. Furthermore, to address the insufficient capability of existing multi-temporal networks in modeling spatio-spectral dynamics and cross-dimensional interactions, we design the Spectral–Spatial Mamba network (SSMamba). Built upon a structured state space model, SSMamba adopts dual spectral and spatial branches tailored for multi-temporal inputs, achieving coordinated representation learning and efficient feature interaction. This design allows for fully exploiting spectral consistency and spatial dependencies across temporal observations, leading to improved detail restoration and stronger generalization. Experimental results on multiple real-image scenes demonstrate that the proposed framework not only achieves accurate reconstruction of cloud-covered regions but also delivers substantial advantages over state-of-the-art approaches, highlighting its strong potential for operational applications. Published: 2026-02-21T14:02:48+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing of Environment Score: 0.803 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chengyao Zhang; Fengyan Wang; Xuqing Zhang; Mingchang Wang; Feng Chen; Xiang Wu

Journal: Remote Sensing of Environment

DOI: 10.1016/j.rse.2026.115321

Relevance: 0.803 (must_read)

Developing a practical and robust cloud removal method has long been a crucial research objective in the field of remote sensing. Existing multi-temporal approaches typically adopt cloud-free images from other acquisition dates as training targets. However, the inherent temporal heterogeneity of optical observations introduces intrinsic limitations to this paradigm. To overcome these issues, we propose a novel cloud removal strategy based on a Generate-to-Remove (G2R) self-supervised framework. Unlike conventional methods, G2R employs the cloudy image itself as the supervision target, thereby introducing temporal constraints into the reconstruction process and enabling faithful recovery of the original surface features beneath cloud-covered regions. Furthermore, to address the insufficient capability of existing multi-temporal networks in modeling spatio-spectral dynamics and cross-dimensional interactions, we design the Spectral–Spatial Mamba network (SSMamba). Built upon a structured state space model, SSMamba adopts dual spectral and spatial branches tailored for multi-temporal inputs, achieving coordinated representation learning and efficient feature interaction. This design allows for fully exploiting spectral consistency and spatial dependencies across temporal observations, leading to improved detail restoration and stronger generalization. Experimental results on multiple real-image scenes demonstrate that the proposed framework not only achieves accurate reconstruction of cloud-covered regions but also delivers substantial advantages over state-of-the-art approaches, highlighting its strong potential for operational applications.

item.title: Lightweight modal-guided cross-attention fusion network for visible-infrared object detection
item.dc:creator: Wencong Wu
Hongxi Zhang
Xiuwei Zhang
Hanlin Yin
Yanning Zhang
item.description: Visible-Infrared object detection aims to utilize the complementarity of different modalities to improve the accuracy of object classification and localization in complex environments. However, most existing methods prioritize detection performance while neglecting network complexity, limiting their real-world applications. To this end, we propose a lightweight modal-guided cross-attention fusion network (LCAFNet) for visible-infrared object detection, which is composed of a visible-guided cross-attention block (VG-CAB), an infrared-guided cross-attention block (IG-CAB), and a gated fusion block (GFB). The VG-CAB and IG-CAB utilize attention weights from one modality to guide information aggregation from another modality, enabling cross-modal information interaction and feature fusion from two different perspectives. These two blocks generate complementary features containing visible (VIS) and infrared (IR) information, resulting in a comprehensive and robust multimodal feature representation. Benefiting from the enhanced and complementary features generated by the VG-CAB and IG-CAB, the GFB achieves an adaptive and complete fusion between these features through a gating strategy. Further, shallow VIS and IR features extracted from a dual-branch backbone network are applied to mine and integrate complementary features, which have more spatial and edge information, thus improving the localization and classification capabilities of the detection model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed LCAFNet obtains better detection performance and lower network complexity compared with other excellent models on five commonly used public datasets. Specifically, on the DroneVehicle dataset, our LCAFNet outperforms the state-of-the-art model by 1.6% mAP50, but has only one-eighth the number of network parameters. The source code for our LCAFNet is accessible at https://github.com/WenCongWu/LCAFNet . Published: 2026-02-22T15:20:01+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.799 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wencong Wu; Hongxi Zhang; Xiuwei Zhang; Hanlin Yin; Yanning Zhang

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113350

Relevance: 0.799 (must_read)

Visible-Infrared object detection aims to utilize the complementarity of different modalities to improve the accuracy of object classification and localization in complex environments. However, most existing methods prioritize detection performance while neglecting network complexity, limiting their real-world applications. To this end, we propose a lightweight modal-guided cross-attention fusion network (LCAFNet) for visible-infrared object detection, which is composed of a visible-guided cross-attention block (VG-CAB), an infrared-guided cross-attention block (IG-CAB), and a gated fusion block (GFB). The VG-CAB and IG-CAB utilize attention weights from one modality to guide information aggregation from another modality, enabling cross-modal information interaction and feature fusion from two different perspectives. These two blocks generate complementary features containing visible (VIS) and infrared (IR) information, resulting in a comprehensive and robust multimodal feature representation. Benefiting from the enhanced and complementary features generated by the VG-CAB and IG-CAB, the GFB achieves an adaptive and complete fusion between these features through a gating strategy. Further, shallow VIS and IR features extracted from a dual-branch backbone network are applied to mine and integrate complementary features, which have more spatial and edge information, thus improving the localization and classification capabilities of the detection model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed LCAFNet obtains better detection performance and lower network complexity compared with other excellent models on five commonly used public datasets. Specifically, on the DroneVehicle dataset, our LCAFNet outperforms the state-of-the-art model by 1.6% mAP50, but has only one-eighth the number of network parameters. The source code for our LCAFNet is accessible at https://github.com/WenCongWu/LCAFNet .

item.title: Not All Attention is Needed: Parameter and Computation Efficient Tuning for Multi-modal Large Language Models via Effective Attention Skipping
item.dc:creator: Qiong Wu
Yiyi Zhou
Weihao Ye
Xiaoshuai Sun
Rongrong Ji
item.description: Recently, Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have garnered an influx of interest from both academia and industry. Despite great progress, MLLMs still have a high demand of downstream task tuning for applications, which consumes excessive parameter and computation overhead. In this paper, we propose a novel parameter and computation efficient tuning method for MLLMs, termed Effective Attention Skipping (EAS). Concretely, we first reveal that multi-head attentions (MHAs) in MLLMs, the primary source of computation, are often redundant to downstream tasks. Based on this observation, EAS evaluates attention redundancy and skips the less important MHAs to speed up inference. Besides, we also propose a novel propagation-of-information adapter (PIA) to serve attention skipping while maintaining parameter efficiency. More importantly, PIA can be further re-parameterized into feed-forward networks (FFNs) for zero-extra latency. To validate EAS, we apply it to three common MLLMs, and conduct extensive experiments on six downstream tasks. The experimental results show that EAS can not only retain the high performance of MLLMs but also reduce the updated parameters scale greatly, while speeding up inference speed to a large extent. For instance, LLaVA-EAS can obtain 96.2% accuracy while accelerating the inference speed by about +20% on ScienceQA. Our code is publicly released at https://github.com/DoubtedSteam/EAS . Published: 2026-02-21T07:39:30+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.798 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qiong Wu; Yiyi Zhou; Weihao Ye; Xiaoshuai Sun; Rongrong Ji

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-025-02702-1

Relevance: 0.798 (must_read)

Recently, Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have garnered an influx of interest from both academia and industry. Despite great progress, MLLMs still have a high demand of downstream task tuning for applications, which consumes excessive parameter and computation overhead. In this paper, we propose a novel parameter and computation efficient tuning method for MLLMs, termed Effective Attention Skipping (EAS). Concretely, we first reveal that multi-head attentions (MHAs) in MLLMs, the primary source of computation, are often redundant to downstream tasks. Based on this observation, EAS evaluates attention redundancy and skips the less important MHAs to speed up inference. Besides, we also propose a novel propagation-of-information adapter (PIA) to serve attention skipping while maintaining parameter efficiency. More importantly, PIA can be further re-parameterized into feed-forward networks (FFNs) for zero-extra latency. To validate EAS, we apply it to three common MLLMs, and conduct extensive experiments on six downstream tasks. The experimental results show that EAS can not only retain the high performance of MLLMs but also reduce the updated parameters scale greatly, while speeding up inference speed to a large extent. For instance, LLaVA-EAS can obtain 96.2% accuracy while accelerating the inference speed by about +20% on ScienceQA. Our code is publicly released at https://github.com/DoubtedSteam/EAS .

item.title: 2Mamba2Furious: Linear in Complexity, Competitive in Accuracy
item.dc:creator: Gabriel Mongaras
Eric C. Larson
item.description: Linear attention transformers have become a strong alternative to softmax attention due to their efficiency. However, linear attention tends to be less expressive and results in reduced accuracy compared to softmax attention. To bridge the accuracy gap between softmax attention and linear attention, we manipulate Mamba-2, a very strong linear attention variant. We first simplify Mamba-2 down to its most fundamental and important components, evaluating which specific choices make it most accurate. From this simplified Mamba variant (Mamba-2S), we improve the A-mask and increase the order of the hidden state, resulting in a method, which we call 2Mamba, that is nearly as accurate as softmax attention, yet much more memory efficient for long context lengths. We also investigate elements to Mamba-2 that help surpass softmax attention accuracy. Code is provided for all our experiments Published: 2026-02-19T13:45:23+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.790 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Gabriel Mongaras; Eric C. Larson

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.790 (must_read)

Linear attention transformers have become a strong alternative to softmax attention due to their efficiency. However, linear attention tends to be less expressive and results in reduced accuracy compared to softmax attention. To bridge the accuracy gap between softmax attention and linear attention, we manipulate Mamba-2, a very strong linear attention variant. We first simplify Mamba-2 down to its most fundamental and important components, evaluating which specific choices make it most accurate. From this simplified Mamba variant (Mamba-2S), we improve the A-mask and increase the order of the hidden state, resulting in a method, which we call 2Mamba, that is nearly as accurate as softmax attention, yet much more memory efficient for long context lengths. We also investigate elements to Mamba-2 that help surpass softmax attention accuracy. Code is provided for all our experiments

item.title: FRCL-SARNet: A Lightweight SAR Ship Detection Network Based on Feature Refinement and Dual Cross Level Fusion
item.dc:creator: Yanjuan Wang
Zhibin Li
Tianmo Wang
Yuxin Hu
Zhaoyan Bai
Gege Ding
Xiongfei Geng
item.description: To address the challenges of small ship targets, strong near-shore clutter, and the difficulty of deploying complex algorithms on terminal devices in SAR images, we propose FRCL-SARNet, an efficient and lightweight detection network. First, a lightweight backbone, LFREBackbone, is designed based on MobileNetV3 and enhanced with a cross-channel local connection mechanism, which strengthens local feature modeling while effectively reducing parameters and computational cost. Second, a dual cross-level feature pyramid network(DCLFPN) is constructed, which introduces high-resolution P2 features and performs multi-layer cross-level fusion to enable efficient interaction between shallow fine-grained and deep semantic features, significantly improving small target detection under complex backgrounds. Furthermore, a Group-Enhanced Attention Mechanism (GEAM) is introduced, combining grouped channel modeling with local context awareness to produce stronger target responses, suppress background interference, and maintain model efficiency. Extensive experiments on three SAR datasets (HRSID, SSDD, and MSAR) show that FRCL-SARNet achieves 91.7% mAP@50 on HRSID with 1.3M parameters and a 3MB model size. Compared with YOLOv8n, FRCL-SARNet reduces parameters by 60% and model size by 49%. Relative to BESW-YOLO, it offers smaller model size, faster inference, and comparable accuracy. These advantages contribute to a strong balance between accuracy, efficiency, and deployability, making FRCL-SARNet especially suitable for real-time deployment on resource-constrained devices in maritime security monitoring and emergency search-and-rescue applications. Published: 2026-02-21T00:25:42+00:00 Venue: Digital Signal Processing Score: 0.788 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yanjuan Wang; Zhibin Li; Tianmo Wang; Yuxin Hu; Zhaoyan Bai; Gege Ding; Xiongfei Geng

Journal: Digital Signal Processing

DOI: 10.1016/j.dsp.2026.106005

Relevance: 0.788 (must_read)

To address the challenges of small ship targets, strong near-shore clutter, and the difficulty of deploying complex algorithms on terminal devices in SAR images, we propose FRCL-SARNet, an efficient and lightweight detection network. First, a lightweight backbone, LFREBackbone, is designed based on MobileNetV3 and enhanced with a cross-channel local connection mechanism, which strengthens local feature modeling while effectively reducing parameters and computational cost. Second, a dual cross-level feature pyramid network(DCLFPN) is constructed, which introduces high-resolution P2 features and performs multi-layer cross-level fusion to enable efficient interaction between shallow fine-grained and deep semantic features, significantly improving small target detection under complex backgrounds. Furthermore, a Group-Enhanced Attention Mechanism (GEAM) is introduced, combining grouped channel modeling with local context awareness to produce stronger target responses, suppress background interference, and maintain model efficiency. Extensive experiments on three SAR datasets (HRSID, SSDD, and MSAR) show that FRCL-SARNet achieves 91.7% mAP@50 on HRSID with 1.3M parameters and a 3MB model size. Compared with YOLOv8n, FRCL-SARNet reduces parameters by 60% and model size by 49%. Relative to BESW-YOLO, it offers smaller model size, faster inference, and comparable accuracy. These advantages contribute to a strong balance between accuracy, efficiency, and deployability, making FRCL-SARNet especially suitable for real-time deployment on resource-constrained devices in maritime security monitoring and emergency search-and-rescue applications.

item.title: Diffusion-Based Data Augmentation for Image Recognition: A Systematic Analysis and Evaluation
item.dc:creator: Zekun Li
Yinghuan Shi
Yang Gao
Dong Xu
item.description: Diffusion-based data augmentation (DiffDA) has emerged as a promising approach to improving classification performance under data scarcity. However, existing works vary significantly in task configurations, model choices, and experimental pipelines, making it difficult to fairly compare methods or assess their effectiveness across different scenarios. Moreover, there remains a lack of systematic understanding of the full DiffDA workflow. In this work, we introduce UniDiffDA, a unified analytical framework that decomposes DiffDA methods into three core components: model fine-tuning, sample generation, and sample utilization. This perspective enables us to identify key differences among existing methods and clarify the overall design space. Building on this framework, we develop a comprehensive and fair evaluation protocol, benchmarking representative DiffDA methods across diverse low-data classification tasks. Extensive experiments reveal the relative strengths and limitations of different DiffDA strategies and offer practical insights into method design and deployment. All methods are re-implemented within a unified codebase, with full release of code and configurations to ensure reproducibility and to facilitate future research. Published: 2026-02-21T07:39:20+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.788 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zekun Li; Yinghuan Shi; Yang Gao; Dong Xu

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02754-x

Relevance: 0.788 (must_read)

Diffusion-based data augmentation (DiffDA) has emerged as a promising approach to improving classification performance under data scarcity. However, existing works vary significantly in task configurations, model choices, and experimental pipelines, making it difficult to fairly compare methods or assess their effectiveness across different scenarios. Moreover, there remains a lack of systematic understanding of the full DiffDA workflow. In this work, we introduce UniDiffDA, a unified analytical framework that decomposes DiffDA methods into three core components: model fine-tuning, sample generation, and sample utilization. This perspective enables us to identify key differences among existing methods and clarify the overall design space. Building on this framework, we develop a comprehensive and fair evaluation protocol, benchmarking representative DiffDA methods across diverse low-data classification tasks. Extensive experiments reveal the relative strengths and limitations of different DiffDA strategies and offer practical insights into method design and deployment. All methods are re-implemented within a unified codebase, with full release of code and configurations to ensure reproducibility and to facilitate future research.

item.title: OmniShift Spatial MixFormer Generalization Network for Cross-Scene Classification of Hyperspectral Image
item.dc:creator: Xingyu Han
Feng Jiang
Shiping Wen
Tianhai Tian
item.description: Domain generalization (DG) plays a vital role in cross-scene hyperspectral image classification (HSIC) as it enables models to generalize using only source domain (SD) data during training. However, existing DG methods still have limitations in generating effective pseudo-images and maintaining generalization in complex scenes. To address these challenges, we propose a novel adversarial learning framework, termed OmniShift Spatial MixFormer Generalization Network (OS²MGnet), for cross-scene HSIC. First, a generator is designed to simulate six realistic domain-shift scenarios—illumination, atmospheric effects, sensor characteristics, phenology, noise, and viewpoint—thereby enhancing generalization to diverse unseen scenarios. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first method that jointly models a comprehensive set of domain shifts grounded in physical remote-sensing principles. Furthermore, a mutual-information consistency supervisor is used to ensure that the generated pseudo-images retain both the semantic labels and domain-invariant features of the SD. Finally, the OS²MGnet discriminator leverages channel-wise interaction learning to extract joint spatial–spectral features, effectively disentangling discriminative, scene-agnostic information from scene-specific features, which contributes to robust performance in complex scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that OS²MGnet achieves exceptional generalization in complex scenarios, even surpassing certain domain adaptation models in accuracy. Published: 2026-02-21T07:18:29+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.787 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xingyu Han; Feng Jiang; Shiping Wen; Tianhai Tian

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.115591

Relevance: 0.787 (must_read)

Domain generalization (DG) plays a vital role in cross-scene hyperspectral image classification (HSIC) as it enables models to generalize using only source domain (SD) data during training. However, existing DG methods still have limitations in generating effective pseudo-images and maintaining generalization in complex scenes. To address these challenges, we propose a novel adversarial learning framework, termed OmniShift Spatial MixFormer Generalization Network (OS²MGnet), for cross-scene HSIC. First, a generator is designed to simulate six realistic domain-shift scenarios—illumination, atmospheric effects, sensor characteristics, phenology, noise, and viewpoint—thereby enhancing generalization to diverse unseen scenarios. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first method that jointly models a comprehensive set of domain shifts grounded in physical remote-sensing principles. Furthermore, a mutual-information consistency supervisor is used to ensure that the generated pseudo-images retain both the semantic labels and domain-invariant features of the SD. Finally, the OS²MGnet discriminator leverages channel-wise interaction learning to extract joint spatial–spectral features, effectively disentangling discriminative, scene-agnostic information from scene-specific features, which contributes to robust performance in complex scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that OS²MGnet achieves exceptional generalization in complex scenarios, even surpassing certain domain adaptation models in accuracy.

item.title: Multi-Modal Primitive Retrieval for Compositional Zero-Shot Learning
item.dc:creator: Chenchen Jing
Haozhe Zhang
Junbo Lu
Yang Liu
Hao Chen
Xiaoqin Zhang
Chunhua Shen
item.description: Compositional generalization, understanding unseen combinations composed of seen primitives, is one of the fundamental properties of human intelligence. Aiming to evaluate such ability of vision models, compositional zero-shot learning (CZSL) requires recognizing unseen attribute-object compositions by learning from seen compositions. It’s essential for CZSL to compose the learned knowledge of seen primitives, i.e., attributes or objects, into novel compositions. In this work, we propose a retrieval-augmented method to explicitly retrieve knowledge of seen primitives from both vision domain and language domain, for compositional zero-shot learning. Our method augments standard multi-path classification methods with retrieval modules. Specifically, we first construct several databases storing abundant and diverse primitive knowledge, including the attribute and object representations of training images, and textual representations for descriptions of attributes and objects, respectively. For an input training/testing image, we use visual and textual retrieval modules to retrieve representations of relevant training images and text descriptions with the same attribute and object, respectively. The primitive representations and image representation of the input image are augmented by using the retrieved representations, for composition recognition. By referencing semantically similar images and texts, the proposed method is capable of recalling knowledge of seen primitives for compositional generalization. Experiments on three widely used datasets show the effectiveness of the proposed method. Published: 2026-02-21T07:39:44+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.786 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chenchen Jing; Haozhe Zhang; Junbo Lu; Yang Liu; Hao Chen; Xiaoqin Zhang; Chunhua Shen

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02763-w

Relevance: 0.786 (must_read)

Compositional generalization, understanding unseen combinations composed of seen primitives, is one of the fundamental properties of human intelligence. Aiming to evaluate such ability of vision models, compositional zero-shot learning (CZSL) requires recognizing unseen attribute-object compositions by learning from seen compositions. It’s essential for CZSL to compose the learned knowledge of seen primitives, i.e., attributes or objects, into novel compositions. In this work, we propose a retrieval-augmented method to explicitly retrieve knowledge of seen primitives from both vision domain and language domain, for compositional zero-shot learning. Our method augments standard multi-path classification methods with retrieval modules. Specifically, we first construct several databases storing abundant and diverse primitive knowledge, including the attribute and object representations of training images, and textual representations for descriptions of attributes and objects, respectively. For an input training/testing image, we use visual and textual retrieval modules to retrieve representations of relevant training images and text descriptions with the same attribute and object, respectively. The primitive representations and image representation of the input image are augmented by using the retrieved representations, for composition recognition. By referencing semantically similar images and texts, the proposed method is capable of recalling knowledge of seen primitives for compositional generalization. Experiments on three widely used datasets show the effectiveness of the proposed method.

item.title: Arcee Trinity Large Technical Report
item.dc:creator: Varun Singh
Lucas Krauss
Sami Jaghouar
Matej Sirovatka
Charles Goddard
Fares Obied
Jack Min Ong
Jannik Straube
Fern
Aria Harley
Conner Stewart
Colin Kealty
Maziyar Panahi
Simon Kirsten
Anushka Deshpande
Anneketh Vij
Arthur Bresnu
Pranav Veldurthi
Raghav Ravishankar
Hardik Bishnoi
DatologyAI Team
Arcee AI Team
Prime Intellect Team
Mark McQuade
Johannes Hagemann
Lucas Atkins
item.description: We present the technical report for Arcee Trinity Large, a sparse Mixture-of-Experts model with 400B total parameters and 13B activated per token. Additionally, we report on Trinity Nano and Trinity Mini, with Trinity Nano having 6B total parameters with 1B activated per token, Trinity Mini having 26B total parameters with 3B activated per token. The models' modern architecture includes interleaved local and global attention, gated attention, depth-scaled sandwich norm, and sigmoid routing for Mixture-of-Experts. For Trinity Large, we also introduce a new MoE load balancing strategy titled Soft-clamped Momentum Expert Bias Updates (SMEBU). We train the models using the Muon optimizer. All three models completed training with zero loss spikes. Trinity Nano and Trinity Mini were pre-trained on 10 trillion tokens, and Trinity Large was pre-trained on 17 trillion tokens. The model checkpoints are available at https://huggingface.co/arcee-ai. Published: 2026-02-19T01:58:50+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.784 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Varun Singh; Lucas Krauss; Sami Jaghouar; Matej Sirovatka; Charles Goddard; Fares Obied; Jack Min Ong; Jannik Straube; Fern; Aria Harley; Conner Stewart; Colin Kealty; Maziyar Panahi; Simon Kirsten; Anushka Deshpande; Anneketh Vij; Arthur Bresnu; Pranav Veldurthi; Raghav Ravishankar; Hardik Bishnoi; DatologyAI Team; Arcee AI Team; Prime Intellect Team; Mark McQuade; Johannes Hagemann; Lucas Atkins

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.784 (must_read)

We present the technical report for Arcee Trinity Large, a sparse Mixture-of-Experts model with 400B total parameters and 13B activated per token. Additionally, we report on Trinity Nano and Trinity Mini, with Trinity Nano having 6B total parameters with 1B activated per token, Trinity Mini having 26B total parameters with 3B activated per token. The models' modern architecture includes interleaved local and global attention, gated attention, depth-scaled sandwich norm, and sigmoid routing for Mixture-of-Experts. For Trinity Large, we also introduce a new MoE load balancing strategy titled Soft-clamped Momentum Expert Bias Updates (SMEBU). We train the models using the Muon optimizer. All three models completed training with zero loss spikes. Trinity Nano and Trinity Mini were pre-trained on 10 trillion tokens, and Trinity Large was pre-trained on 17 trillion tokens. The model checkpoints are available at https://huggingface.co/arcee-ai.

item.title: Selective Training for Large Vision Language Models via Visual Information Gain
item.dc:creator: Seulbi Lee
Sangheum Hwang
item.description: Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) have achieved remarkable progress, yet they often suffer from language bias, producing answers without relying on visual evidence. While prior work attempts to mitigate this issue through decoding strategies, architectural modifications, or curated instruction data, they typically lack a quantitative measure of how much individual training samples or tokens actually benefit from the image. In this work, we introduce Visual Information Gain (VIG), a perplexity-based metric that measures the reduction in prediction uncertainty provided by visual input. VIG enables fine-grained analysis at both sample and token levels, effectively highlighting visually grounded elements such as colors, spatial relations, and attributes. Leveraging this, we propose a VIG-guided selective training scheme that prioritizes high-VIG samples and tokens. This approach improves visual grounding and mitigates language bias, achieving superior performance with significantly reduced supervision by focusing exclusively on visually informative samples and tokens. Published: 2026-02-19T09:12:21+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.777 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Seulbi Lee; Sangheum Hwang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.777 (must_read)

Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) have achieved remarkable progress, yet they often suffer from language bias, producing answers without relying on visual evidence. While prior work attempts to mitigate this issue through decoding strategies, architectural modifications, or curated instruction data, they typically lack a quantitative measure of how much individual training samples or tokens actually benefit from the image. In this work, we introduce Visual Information Gain (VIG), a perplexity-based metric that measures the reduction in prediction uncertainty provided by visual input. VIG enables fine-grained analysis at both sample and token levels, effectively highlighting visually grounded elements such as colors, spatial relations, and attributes. Leveraging this, we propose a VIG-guided selective training scheme that prioritizes high-VIG samples and tokens. This approach improves visual grounding and mitigates language bias, achieving superior performance with significantly reduced supervision by focusing exclusively on visually informative samples and tokens.

item.title: Understanding the Fine-Grained Knowledge Capabilities of Vision-Language Models
item.dc:creator: Dhruba Ghosh
Yuhui Zhang
Ludwig Schmidt
item.description: Vision-language models (VLMs) have made substantial progress across a wide range of visual question answering benchmarks, spanning visual reasoning, document understanding, and multimodal dialogue. These improvements are evident in a wide range of VLMs built on a variety of base models, alignment architectures, and training data. However, recent works show that these models trail behind in traditional image classification benchmarks, which test fine-grained visual knowledge. We test a large number of recent VLMs on fine-grained classification benchmarks and identify potential factors in the disconnect between fine-grained knowledge and other vision benchmarks. Through a series of ablation experiments, we find that using a better LLM improves all benchmark scores equally, while a better vision encoder disproportionately improves fine-grained classification performance. Furthermore, we find that the pretraining stage is also vital to fine-grained performance, particularly when the language model weights are unfrozen during pretraining. These insights pave the way for enhancing fine-grained visual understanding and vision-centric capabilities in VLMs. Published: 2026-02-19T22:07:29+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.777 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Dhruba Ghosh; Yuhui Zhang; Ludwig Schmidt

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.777 (must_read)

Vision-language models (VLMs) have made substantial progress across a wide range of visual question answering benchmarks, spanning visual reasoning, document understanding, and multimodal dialogue. These improvements are evident in a wide range of VLMs built on a variety of base models, alignment architectures, and training data. However, recent works show that these models trail behind in traditional image classification benchmarks, which test fine-grained visual knowledge. We test a large number of recent VLMs on fine-grained classification benchmarks and identify potential factors in the disconnect between fine-grained knowledge and other vision benchmarks. Through a series of ablation experiments, we find that using a better LLM improves all benchmark scores equally, while a better vision encoder disproportionately improves fine-grained classification performance. Furthermore, we find that the pretraining stage is also vital to fine-grained performance, particularly when the language model weights are unfrozen during pretraining. These insights pave the way for enhancing fine-grained visual understanding and vision-centric capabilities in VLMs.

item.title: MTTrack: A Joint Mamba-Transformer Framework with Memory Enhancement for Real-time Satellite Remote Sensing Video Object Tracking
item.dc:creator: Guocai Du
Peiyong Zhou
Nurbiya Yadikar
Alimjan Aysa
Kurban Ubul
item.description: The existing object tracking methods face major challenges in satellite remote sensing video, mainly due to the small size of the object, the insignificant appearance characteristics and the frequent occlusion phenomenon. In addition, many methods often fail to make full use of historical frames and template information, thus increasing the computational burden. To address these issues, we proposes a novel joint mamba-transformer framework with memory enhancement for real-time satellite remote sensing video object tracking (MTTrack). This framework combines the advantages of transformer in modeling long-range dependencies with the strengths of mamba architecture in efficient computing. Firstly, MTTrack effectively captures complex intra group dependencies and generates precise group embedding representations through the designed with-group transformer module. Secondly, an efficient cross-group mamba architecture is proposed, which can effectively handle inter group relationships and comprehensively extract effective visual features. In addition, to address the challenges posed by random sorting in the mamba architecture, we have introduced a dual-path weight-aware ordering strategy. This strategy reorders group embeddings based on weight scores, significantly improving the performance of mamba. Finally, MTTrack introduces a diffusion model in the decoder to effectively handle the occlusion and appearance deformation of objects in complex scenes, thereby further improving the accuracy of object localization. The experimental results show that the performance on four remote sensing video datasets is superior to the existing state-of-the-art methods, verifying the effectiveness and robustness of MTTrack. Published: 2026-02-22T06:44:39+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.776 (must_read)

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.115572

Relevance: 0.776 (must_read)

The existing object tracking methods face major challenges in satellite remote sensing video, mainly due to the small size of the object, the insignificant appearance characteristics and the frequent occlusion phenomenon. In addition, many methods often fail to make full use of historical frames and template information, thus increasing the computational burden. To address these issues, we proposes a novel joint mamba-transformer framework with memory enhancement for real-time satellite remote sensing video object tracking (MTTrack). This framework combines the advantages of transformer in modeling long-range dependencies with the strengths of mamba architecture in efficient computing. Firstly, MTTrack effectively captures complex intra group dependencies and generates precise group embedding representations through the designed with-group transformer module. Secondly, an efficient cross-group mamba architecture is proposed, which can effectively handle inter group relationships and comprehensively extract effective visual features. In addition, to address the challenges posed by random sorting in the mamba architecture, we have introduced a dual-path weight-aware ordering strategy. This strategy reorders group embeddings based on weight scores, significantly improving the performance of mamba. Finally, MTTrack introduces a diffusion model in the decoder to effectively handle the occlusion and appearance deformation of objects in complex scenes, thereby further improving the accuracy of object localization. The experimental results show that the performance on four remote sensing video datasets is superior to the existing state-of-the-art methods, verifying the effectiveness and robustness of MTTrack.

item.title: Degradation Decomposition Learning for Self-supervised Blind Image Super-resolution
item.dc:creator: Hongyang Zhou
Xiaobin Zhu
Liuling Chen
Xiaoxing Zhang
Jingyan Qin
Xu-cheng Yin
item.description: Existing image super-resolution (SR) methods often rely on implicit or explicit estimation of real-world degradation, yet they suffer significant performance drops when the low-resolution (LR) degradation is unseen during training. Inspired by generalized sampling theory, some approaches attempt to enhance adaptability by mapping LR images to a known degradation. However, their effectiveness is restricted by inaccurate degradation estimation. To overcome this limitation, we propose an adaptive blind SR framework based on a novel degradation decomposition strategy. Instead of directly estimating degradation, our method decomposes the unknown degraded LR input into a known degraded LR image, a degradation difference, and a difference-aware filter. The known degraded LR image is aligned with an image-specific SR model trained in a zero-shot manner with known degradation, while the degradation difference and difference-aware filter serve as constraints that facilitate decomposition. To realize this, we design a degradation decomposition network (DDN), which leverages the decomposed components as supervised signals for self-supervised training. This design enables our method to efficiently adapt unknown degraded LR images to the image-specific SR model without requiring explicit degradation estimation. Extensive experiments on synthetic datasets and real-world images demonstrate the superior performance of our method. Published: 2026-02-21T16:08:16+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.775 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hongyang Zhou; Xiaobin Zhu; Liuling Chen; Xiaoxing Zhang; Jingyan Qin; Xu-cheng Yin

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113340

Relevance: 0.775 (must_read)

Existing image super-resolution (SR) methods often rely on implicit or explicit estimation of real-world degradation, yet they suffer significant performance drops when the low-resolution (LR) degradation is unseen during training. Inspired by generalized sampling theory, some approaches attempt to enhance adaptability by mapping LR images to a known degradation. However, their effectiveness is restricted by inaccurate degradation estimation. To overcome this limitation, we propose an adaptive blind SR framework based on a novel degradation decomposition strategy. Instead of directly estimating degradation, our method decomposes the unknown degraded LR input into a known degraded LR image, a degradation difference, and a difference-aware filter. The known degraded LR image is aligned with an image-specific SR model trained in a zero-shot manner with known degradation, while the degradation difference and difference-aware filter serve as constraints that facilitate decomposition. To realize this, we design a degradation decomposition network (DDN), which leverages the decomposed components as supervised signals for self-supervised training. This design enables our method to efficiently adapt unknown degraded LR images to the image-specific SR model without requiring explicit degradation estimation. Extensive experiments on synthetic datasets and real-world images demonstrate the superior performance of our method.

item.title: Pose without Guesses: Generalizable Object Pose Estimation From a Single Reference
item.dc:creator: Chen Zhao
Tong Zhang
Zheng Dang
Mathieu Salzmann
item.description: Determining the relative pose of a previously unseen object between two images is pivotal to the success of generalizable object pose estimation. Existing approaches typically predict 3D translation utilizing the ground-truth object bounding box and approximate 3D rotation with a large number of discrete hypotheses. This strategy makes unrealistic assumptions about the availability of ground truth and incurs a computationally expensive process of scoring each hypothesis at test time. By contrast, we rethink the problem of relative pose estimation for unseen objects by presenting an advanced Deep Voxel Matching Network (DVMNet++). Our method computes the relative object pose in a single pass, eliminating the need for ground-truth object bounding boxes and rotation hypotheses. We achieve open-set object detection by leveraging image feature embedding and natural language understanding as reference. The detection result is then employed to approximate the translation parameters and crop the object from the query image. For rotation estimation, we map the reference and cropped query to their respective voxelized 3D representations. The resulting voxels are passed through a rotation estimation module, which aligns the voxels and computes the rotation in an end-to-end fashion by solving a least-squares problem. To enhance robustness, we introduce a weighted closest voxel algorithm capable of mitigating the impact of noisy voxels. We conduct extensive experiments on the CO3D, Objaverse, LINEMOD, HomebrewedDB, and LINEMOD-O datasets, demonstrating that our approach delivers more accurate relative pose estimates for novel objects at a lower computational cost compared to state-of-the-art methods. Our code is released at https://github.com/sailor-z/DVMNet/ . Published: 2026-02-22T10:08:42+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.775 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chen Zhao; Tong Zhang; Zheng Dang; Mathieu Salzmann

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02781-8

Relevance: 0.775 (must_read)

Determining the relative pose of a previously unseen object between two images is pivotal to the success of generalizable object pose estimation. Existing approaches typically predict 3D translation utilizing the ground-truth object bounding box and approximate 3D rotation with a large number of discrete hypotheses. This strategy makes unrealistic assumptions about the availability of ground truth and incurs a computationally expensive process of scoring each hypothesis at test time. By contrast, we rethink the problem of relative pose estimation for unseen objects by presenting an advanced Deep Voxel Matching Network (DVMNet++). Our method computes the relative object pose in a single pass, eliminating the need for ground-truth object bounding boxes and rotation hypotheses. We achieve open-set object detection by leveraging image feature embedding and natural language understanding as reference. The detection result is then employed to approximate the translation parameters and crop the object from the query image. For rotation estimation, we map the reference and cropped query to their respective voxelized 3D representations. The resulting voxels are passed through a rotation estimation module, which aligns the voxels and computes the rotation in an end-to-end fashion by solving a least-squares problem. To enhance robustness, we introduce a weighted closest voxel algorithm capable of mitigating the impact of noisy voxels. We conduct extensive experiments on the CO3D, Objaverse, LINEMOD, HomebrewedDB, and LINEMOD-O datasets, demonstrating that our approach delivers more accurate relative pose estimates for novel objects at a lower computational cost compared to state-of-the-art methods. Our code is released at https://github.com/sailor-z/DVMNet/ .

item.title: WS-GRPO: Weakly-Supervised Group-Relative Policy Optimization for Rollout-Efficient Reasoning
item.dc:creator: Gagan Mundada
Zihan Huang
Rohan Surana
Sheldon Yu
Jennifer Yuntong Zhang
Xintong Li
Tong Yu
Lina Yao
Jingbo Shang
Julian McAuley
Junda Wu
item.description: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is effective for training language models on complex reasoning. However, since the objective is defined relative to a group of sampled trajectories, extended deliberation can create more chances to realize relative gains, leading to inefficient reasoning and overthinking, and complicating the trade-off between correctness and rollout efficiency. Controlling this behavior is difficult in practice, considering (i) Length penalties are hard to calibrate because longer rollouts may reflect harder problems that require longer reasoning, penalizing tokens risks truncating useful reasoning along with redundant continuation; and (ii) supervision that directly indicates when to continue or stop is typically unavailable beyond final answer correctness. We propose Weakly Supervised GRPO (WS-GRPO), which improves rollout efficiency by converting terminal rewards into correctness-aware guidance over partial trajectories. Unlike global length penalties that are hard to calibrate, WS-GRPO trains a preference model from outcome-only correctness to produce prefix-level signals that indicate when additional continuation is beneficial. Thus, WS-GRPO supplies outcome-derived continue/stop guidance, reducing redundant deliberation while maintaining accuracy. We provide theoretical results and empirically show on reasoning benchmarks that WS-GRPO substantially reduces rollout length while remaining competitive with GRPO baselines. Published: 2026-02-19T02:43:35+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.775 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Gagan Mundada; Zihan Huang; Rohan Surana; Sheldon Yu; Jennifer Yuntong Zhang; Xintong Li; Tong Yu; Lina Yao; Jingbo Shang; Julian McAuley; Junda Wu

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.775 (must_read)

Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is effective for training language models on complex reasoning. However, since the objective is defined relative to a group of sampled trajectories, extended deliberation can create more chances to realize relative gains, leading to inefficient reasoning and overthinking, and complicating the trade-off between correctness and rollout efficiency. Controlling this behavior is difficult in practice, considering (i) Length penalties are hard to calibrate because longer rollouts may reflect harder problems that require longer reasoning, penalizing tokens risks truncating useful reasoning along with redundant continuation; and (ii) supervision that directly indicates when to continue or stop is typically unavailable beyond final answer correctness. We propose Weakly Supervised GRPO (WS-GRPO), which improves rollout efficiency by converting terminal rewards into correctness-aware guidance over partial trajectories. Unlike global length penalties that are hard to calibrate, WS-GRPO trains a preference model from outcome-only correctness to produce prefix-level signals that indicate when additional continuation is beneficial. Thus, WS-GRPO supplies outcome-derived continue/stop guidance, reducing redundant deliberation while maintaining accuracy. We provide theoretical results and empirically show on reasoning benchmarks that WS-GRPO substantially reduces rollout length while remaining competitive with GRPO baselines.

item.title: Physical Regularization Loss: Integrating Physical Knowledge to Image Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Yan Ding
Shuang Li
Huafeng Li
Guanqiu Qi
Baisen Cong
Yunpeng Gong
Zhiqin Zhu
item.description: This paper presents a novel image segmentation optimization approach that incorporates physical constraints through anisotropic diffusion equations during the segmentation process. It points out that most current models rely on data-driven approaches to perform image segmentation tasks, requiring large amounts of high-quality training data. When faced with limited datasets or significant domain shifts, these models often struggle to learn comprehensive features, affecting segmentation accuracy. Moreover, these models often require dataset-specific architectural modifications, resulting in inconsistent cross-dataset performance. To resolve this issue, this paper proposes a novel method incorporating a new regulator derived from anisotropic diffusion equations which is designated as the physical regulation loss (PRL) function . Through constraints of PRL based on observable results, PRL enhances image segmentation models’ extrapolation and generalization capabilities, improving their overall performance. The experiment results prove the theoretical feasibility of PRL, and this paper also presents an efficient numerical algorithm for calculation. The proposed method demonstrates improvements across diverse segmentation tasks, including urban scene analysis Cityscapes, natural image processing datasets Pascal VOC 201, and medical imaging applications such as dermatology datasets ISIC 2017 & 2018, and brain tumor segmentation datasets BraTS 2020. Published: 2026-02-22T14:42:01+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.775 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yan Ding; Shuang Li; Huafeng Li; Guanqiu Qi; Baisen Cong; Yunpeng Gong; Zhiqin Zhu

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02776-5

Relevance: 0.775 (must_read)

This paper presents a novel image segmentation optimization approach that incorporates physical constraints through anisotropic diffusion equations during the segmentation process. It points out that most current models rely on data-driven approaches to perform image segmentation tasks, requiring large amounts of high-quality training data. When faced with limited datasets or significant domain shifts, these models often struggle to learn comprehensive features, affecting segmentation accuracy. Moreover, these models often require dataset-specific architectural modifications, resulting in inconsistent cross-dataset performance. To resolve this issue, this paper proposes a novel method incorporating a new regulator derived from anisotropic diffusion equations which is designated as the physical regulation loss (PRL) function . Through constraints of PRL based on observable results, PRL enhances image segmentation models’ extrapolation and generalization capabilities, improving their overall performance. The experiment results prove the theoretical feasibility of PRL, and this paper also presents an efficient numerical algorithm for calculation. The proposed method demonstrates improvements across diverse segmentation tasks, including urban scene analysis Cityscapes, natural image processing datasets Pascal VOC 201, and medical imaging applications such as dermatology datasets ISIC 2017 & 2018, and brain tumor segmentation datasets BraTS 2020.

item.title: KLong: Training LLM Agent for Extremely Long-horizon Tasks
item.dc:creator: Yue Liu
Zhiyuan Hu
Flood Sung
Jiaheng Zhang
Bryan Hooi
item.description: This paper introduces KLong, an open-source LLM agent trained to solve extremely long-horizon tasks. The principle is to first cold-start the model via trajectory-splitting SFT, then scale it via progressive RL training. Specifically, we first activate basic agentic abilities of a base model with a comprehensive SFT recipe. Then, we introduce Research-Factory, an automated pipeline that generates high-quality training data by collecting research papers and constructing evaluation rubrics. Using this pipeline, we build thousands of long-horizon trajectories distilled from Claude 4.5 Sonnet (Thinking). To train with these extremely long trajectories, we propose a new trajectory-splitting SFT, which preserves early context, progressively truncates later context, and maintains overlap between sub-trajectories. In addition, to further improve long-horizon task-solving capability, we propose a novel progressive RL, which schedules training into multiple stages with progressively extended timeouts. Experiments demonstrate the superiority and generalization of KLong, as shown in Figure 1. Notably, our proposed KLong (106B) surpasses Kimi K2 Thinking (1T) by 11.28% on PaperBench, and the performance improvement generalizes to other coding benchmarks like SWE-bench Verified and MLE-bench. Published: 2026-02-19T17:01:08+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.773 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yue Liu; Zhiyuan Hu; Flood Sung; Jiaheng Zhang; Bryan Hooi

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.773 (must_read)

This paper introduces KLong, an open-source LLM agent trained to solve extremely long-horizon tasks. The principle is to first cold-start the model via trajectory-splitting SFT, then scale it via progressive RL training. Specifically, we first activate basic agentic abilities of a base model with a comprehensive SFT recipe. Then, we introduce Research-Factory, an automated pipeline that generates high-quality training data by collecting research papers and constructing evaluation rubrics. Using this pipeline, we build thousands of long-horizon trajectories distilled from Claude 4.5 Sonnet (Thinking). To train with these extremely long trajectories, we propose a new trajectory-splitting SFT, which preserves early context, progressively truncates later context, and maintains overlap between sub-trajectories. In addition, to further improve long-horizon task-solving capability, we propose a novel progressive RL, which schedules training into multiple stages with progressively extended timeouts. Experiments demonstrate the superiority and generalization of KLong, as shown in Figure 1. Notably, our proposed KLong (106B) surpasses Kimi K2 Thinking (1T) by 11.28% on PaperBench, and the performance improvement generalizes to other coding benchmarks like SWE-bench Verified and MLE-bench.

item.title: InfoCAM: An Information-Weighted Class Activation Mapping for Explaining Visual Neural Networks
item.dc:creator: Yulong Shi
Mingwei Sun
Zengqiang Chen
item.description: With the rapid advancement of deep learning technologies, visual neural networks have made significant progress across various benchmarks. However, these networks heavily rely on nonlinear functions and hyperparameter tuning techniques, which leads to black-box behavior during forward inference. To enhance the transparency of decision-making, the information flow in visual neural networks is revisited, and a saliency visual explanation framework called Information-weighted Class Activation Mapping (InfoCAM) is proposed. The InfoCAM regards deep visual neural networks as complex information processing systems and constructs a Dual-Stream Information Bottleneck (DSIB) module. DSIB adopts a divide-and-conquer strategy by introducing two latent variables and employs variational inference to explicitly decompose intermediate feature activations into a discriminative feature stream and a task-irrelevant noise stream. Through maximizing the mutual information between the discriminative stream and the output prediction, the weighting coefficients are faithfully assigned to feature activation maps, thereby generating reliable visual explanations. InfoCAM is not affected by the shattered gradient problem and can be seamlessly integrated into various visual network architectures and computer vision tasks. Furthermore, the average drop and average increase metrics used for faithfulness evaluation are improved by explicitly clarifying their theoretical bounds and mitigating their sensitivity to fluctuations in network output scores. Experimental results show that InfoCAM can achieve faithful explanation of visual neural networks, demonstrating superior performance in perturbation testing and energy-based pointing game evaluations. Published: 2026-02-21T16:13:13+00:00 Venue: Neural Networks Score: 0.772 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yulong Shi; Mingwei Sun; Zengqiang Chen

Journal: Neural Networks

DOI: 10.1016/j.neunet.2026.108757

Relevance: 0.772 (must_read)

With the rapid advancement of deep learning technologies, visual neural networks have made significant progress across various benchmarks. However, these networks heavily rely on nonlinear functions and hyperparameter tuning techniques, which leads to black-box behavior during forward inference. To enhance the transparency of decision-making, the information flow in visual neural networks is revisited, and a saliency visual explanation framework called Information-weighted Class Activation Mapping (InfoCAM) is proposed. The InfoCAM regards deep visual neural networks as complex information processing systems and constructs a Dual-Stream Information Bottleneck (DSIB) module. DSIB adopts a divide-and-conquer strategy by introducing two latent variables and employs variational inference to explicitly decompose intermediate feature activations into a discriminative feature stream and a task-irrelevant noise stream. Through maximizing the mutual information between the discriminative stream and the output prediction, the weighting coefficients are faithfully assigned to feature activation maps, thereby generating reliable visual explanations. InfoCAM is not affected by the shattered gradient problem and can be seamlessly integrated into various visual network architectures and computer vision tasks. Furthermore, the average drop and average increase metrics used for faithfulness evaluation are improved by explicitly clarifying their theoretical bounds and mitigating their sensitivity to fluctuations in network output scores. Experimental results show that InfoCAM can achieve faithful explanation of visual neural networks, demonstrating superior performance in perturbation testing and energy-based pointing game evaluations.

item.title: MASPO: Unifying Gradient Utilization, Probability Mass, and Signal Reliability for Robust and Sample-Efficient LLM Reasoning
item.dc:creator: Xiaoliang Fu
Jiaye Lin
Yangyi Fang
Binbin Zheng
Chaowen Hu
Zekai Shao
Cong Qin
Lu Pan
Ke Zeng
Xunliang Cai
item.description: Existing Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) algorithms, such as GRPO, rely on rigid, uniform, and symmetric trust region mechanisms that are fundamentally misaligned with the complex optimization dynamics of Large Language Models (LLMs). In this paper, we identify three critical challenges in these methods: (1) inefficient gradient utilization caused by the binary cutoff of hard clipping, (2) insensitive probability mass arising from uniform ratio constraints that ignore the token distribution, and (3) asymmetric signal reliability stemming from the disparate credit assignment ambiguity between positive and negative samples. To bridge these gaps, we propose Mass-Adaptive Soft Policy Optimization (MASPO), a unified framework designed to harmonize these three dimensions. MASPO integrates a differentiable soft Gaussian gating to maximize gradient utility, a mass-adaptive limiter to balance exploration across the probability spectrum, and an asymmetric risk controller to align update magnitudes with signal confidence. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that MASPO serves as a robust, all-in-one RLVR solution, significantly outperforming strong baselines. Our code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ma1/README.md. Published: 2026-02-19T17:05:20+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.772 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiaoliang Fu; Jiaye Lin; Yangyi Fang; Binbin Zheng; Chaowen Hu; Zekai Shao; Cong Qin; Lu Pan; Ke Zeng; Xunliang Cai

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.772 (must_read)

Existing Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) algorithms, such as GRPO, rely on rigid, uniform, and symmetric trust region mechanisms that are fundamentally misaligned with the complex optimization dynamics of Large Language Models (LLMs). In this paper, we identify three critical challenges in these methods: (1) inefficient gradient utilization caused by the binary cutoff of hard clipping, (2) insensitive probability mass arising from uniform ratio constraints that ignore the token distribution, and (3) asymmetric signal reliability stemming from the disparate credit assignment ambiguity between positive and negative samples. To bridge these gaps, we propose Mass-Adaptive Soft Policy Optimization (MASPO), a unified framework designed to harmonize these three dimensions. MASPO integrates a differentiable soft Gaussian gating to maximize gradient utility, a mass-adaptive limiter to balance exploration across the probability spectrum, and an asymmetric risk controller to align update magnitudes with signal confidence. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that MASPO serves as a robust, all-in-one RLVR solution, significantly outperforming strong baselines. Our code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ma1/README.md.

item.title: A Survey of Multimodal Hallucination Evaluation and Detection
item.dc:creator: Zhiyuan Chen
Yuecong Min
Jie Zhang
Bei Yan
Jiahao Wang
Xiaozhen Wang
Shiguang Shan
item.description: Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for integrating visual and textual information, supporting a wide range of multi-modal tasks. However, these models often suffer from hallucination, producing content that appears plausible but contradicts the input content or established world knowledge. This survey offers an in-depth review of hallucination evaluation benchmarks and detection methods across Image-to-Text (I2T) and Text-to-image (T2I) generation tasks. Specifically, we first propose a taxonomy of hallucination based on faithfulness and factuality, incorporating the common types of hallucinations observed in practice. Then we provide an overview of existing hallucination evaluation benchmarks for both T2I and I2T tasks, highlighting their construction process, evaluation objectives, and employed metrics. Furthermore, we summarize recent advances in hallucination detection methods, which aims to identify hallucinated content at the instance level and serve as a practical complement of benchmark-based evaluation. Finally, we highlight key limitations in current benchmarks and detection methods, and outline potential directions for future research. Published: 2026-02-21T07:39:28+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.772 (consider)
